results.prompt: What are the most important changes in the EU AI Act that take effect in 2025? Summarize with sources.
results.agent:
## Key EU AI Act Changes Taking Effect in 2025

The EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689) entered into force on August 1, 2024, but its provisions roll out on a staggered timeline. Three major compliance milestones occurred in 2025:

---

### 1. February 2, 2025 — Prohibited AI Practices & AI Literacy

This was the Act's first enforcement deadline. Two chapters became applicable:

**Prohibited Practices (Article 5):** Eight categories of AI use are now outright banned across the EU:

- **Subliminal or manipulative/deceptive AI** that materially distorts human behavior and causes significant harm
- **Exploitation of vulnerabilities** (age, disability, socio-economic situation) to distort behavior
- **Social scoring** — classifying people based on behavior or personal traits in unrelated contexts (e.g., using a bad driving record to deny financial services), leading to discriminatory or exclusionary treatment
- **Criminal risk prediction** (predictive policing based solely on profiling)
- **Untargeted facial-image scraping** to create facial recognition databases from the internet or CCTV
- **Emotion recognition** in workplaces and educational institutions (with exceptions for medical/safety purposes)
- **Biometric categorization** using sensitive characteristics (race, religion, health, etc.)
- **Real-time remote biometric identification** in public spaces for law enforcement, with narrow exceptions (e.g., searching for missing persons, preventing imminent terrorist threats)

**AI Literacy (Article 4):** All providers and deployers of AI systems must ensure their personnel have sufficient AI literacy to operate those systems responsibly.

**Penalties:** Violations of prohibited practices can incur fines of up to €35 million or 7% of global annual turnover (though the penalty provisions themselves became enforceable on August 2, 2025).

*Sources: [DLA Piper](https://www.dlapiper.com/insights/publications/ai-outlook/2025/eu-ai-acts-ban-on-prohibited-practices-takes-effect); [Jones Day](https://www.jonesday.com/en/insights/2025/02/eu-ai-act-first-rules-take-effect-on-prohibited-ai-systems); [EY Law Alert](https://www.ey.com/content/dam/ey-unified-site/ey-com/en-gr/technical/tax/tax-alerts/2025/02/ai-act-en-v3.pdf); [Mayer Brown](https://www.mayerbrown.com/en/insights/publications/2025/01/eu-ai-act-ban-on-certain-ai-practices-and-requirements-for-ai-literacy-come-into-effect); [artificialintelligenceact.eu – Article 5](https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/article/5)*

---

### 2. May 2, 2025 — Codes of Practice for GPAI Models

By this date, the European AI Office was required to publish **Codes of Practice** giving guidance to providers of general-purpose AI (GPAI) models on how to meet their obligations under the Act (Article 56).

*Source: [DLA Piper](https://www.dlapiper.com/insights/publications/ai-outlook/2025/eu-ai-acts-ban-on-prohibited-practices-takes-effect); [artificialintelligenceact.eu – Implementation Timeline](https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/implementation-timeline)*

---

### 3. August 2, 2025 — GPAI Obligations, Governance & Sanctions

The largest single wave of 2025 requirements took effect on this date:

**GPAI Model Obligations (Chapter V, Articles 52–55):**

- **All GPAI providers** must maintain technical documentation (model architecture, training data, compute), provide downstream documentation to AI system providers who integrate their models, implement a copyright compliance policy, and publish a summary of training data content.
- **Systemic-risk GPAI models** face additional obligations. A model is presumed to pose systemic risk if trained using more than **10²⁵ FLOPs** of cumulative compute (Article 51(2)); the Commission can also designate models as systemic risk on its own initiative. Systemic-risk providers must conduct **adversarial testing and red-teaming**, perform **systemic-risk assessment and mitigation**, report **serious incidents** to the AI Office, and implement **cybersecurity measures**.

**Governance Structures (Chapter VII):** Member States were required to designate **national competent authorities** for AI oversight and enforcement. The European AI Office became fully operational for GPAI supervision.

**Sanctions Provisions:** The penalty framework for prohibited-practice violations and GPAI non-compliance became enforceable on this date.

*Sources: [European Commission – Digital Strategy](https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/policies/regulatory-framework-ai); [McCann Fitzgerald](https://www.mccannfitzgerald.com/knowledge/data-privacy-and-cyber-risk/eu-ai-act-enters-into-force-key-compliance-dates-for-stakeholders); [artificialintelligenceact.eu – Article 51](https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/article/51); [Modulos Docs](https://docs.modulos.ai/frameworks/eu-ai-act/general-purpose-ai-models); [Nemko Digital](https://digital.nemko.com/insights/eu-ai-act-rules-on-gpai-2025-update)*

---

### What's Still Ahead

| Date | Milestone |
|---|---|
| **August 2, 2026** | High-risk AI system requirements (conformity assessments, CE marking, risk management, human oversight) |
| **August 2, 2027** | AI embedded in regulated products (medical devices, machinery, vehicles); legacy GPAI models must reach full compliance |

---

### Summary

In 2025, the EU AI Act moved from law-on-the-books to active enforcement in two major steps: **banning the most harmful AI practices** (February) and **imposing transparency and governance rules on general-purpose AI models** (August). Companies operating in or affecting the EU market must now be in compliance with both sets of requirements, with significant financial penalties for non-compliance.
results.claude:
I should flag up front that I can't verify live sources here, so treat the specifics below as my best understanding rather than citable fact — check the official text before relying on it.

The EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689) entered into force on 1 August 2024 and applies in phases. The key milestones that take effect in 2025 are:

1. Prohibited practices (from 2 February 2025): The first set of obligations to apply are the outright bans on "unacceptable risk" AI. These include social scoring by public authorities, manipulative or subliminal techniques that cause harm, exploitation of vulnerabilities (age, disability, economic situation), untargeted scraping of facial images to build recognition databases, emotion recognition in workplaces and schools, certain biometric categorisation of sensitive traits, and most real-time remote biometric identification in public spaces by law enforcement (with narrow exceptions).

2. AI literacy obligation (from 2 February 2025): Providers and deployers must take measures to ensure a sufficient level of AI literacy among staff and others operating AI systems on their behalf.

3. General-purpose AI (GPAI) model rules (from 2 August 2025): Obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models begin to apply — including technical documentation, transparency to downstream providers, a policy to comply with EU copyright law, and a summary of training content. Models posing "systemic risk" face added duties (model evaluation, adversarial testing, incident reporting, cybersecurity). A voluntary GPAI Code of Practice was developed to help demonstrate compliance.

4. Governance and penalties (from 2 August 2025): The governance architecture (the AI Office, the AI Board, designation of national competent authorities) and the penalty provisions become applicable. Fines can reach up to 7% of global annual turnover (or EUR 35 million) for prohibited-practice violations, with lower tiers for other breaches.

Note on phasing beyond 2025: most high-risk system requirements apply later (broadly 2 August 2026, with certain product-embedded high-risk systems by 2 August 2027).

Suggested primary sources to confirm these details: the consolidated regulation on EUR-Lex (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689); the European Commission's "AI Act" pages and the EU AI Office; and the official Code of Practice for general-purpose AI. I'd verify the exact dated obligations against EUR-Lex, since the staggered timeline is where errors creep in.

results.prompt:
Rewrite these into 3 strong resume bullets. Use ONLY these facts, do not invent metrics:
- maintained internal Python tooling
- helped migrate CI to GitHub Actions
- mentored 2 junior engineers
results.agent:
Here are three resume bullets using only the facts you provided:

- **Maintained internal Python tooling**, ensuring reliability and usability across engineering workflows.
- **Helped migrate CI pipelines to GitHub Actions**, contributing to a more streamlined and modern deployment process.
- **Mentored two junior engineers**, providing technical guidance and supporting their onboarding and skill development.
results.claude:
- Built and maintained internal Python tooling used by the engineering team to support day-to-day development workflows.
- Helped migrate the team's continuous integration pipeline to GitHub Actions, modernizing the build and test process.
- Mentored two junior engineers, supporting their onboarding and technical growth.

glm_judge.why: Both provide three accurate bullets, clear language, no invented metrics, and meet the request equally well.

results.prompt: Write a short cover letter for a data analyst role at a fintech. Candidate facts: 3 years SQL, dashboards in Tableau, A/B testing experience.
results.agent:
Here's a short, polished cover letter:

---

Dear Hiring Manager,

I'm excited to apply for the Data Analyst role at [Company Name]. With three years of hands-on experience in SQL, I've developed a strong foundation in querying and transforming complex datasets to drive business decisions — a skill I'm eager to bring to the fast-paced fintech space.

In my current role, I design and maintain dashboards in Tableau that give stakeholders clear, real-time visibility into key metrics. I also have direct experience designing and analyzing A/B tests, from hypothesis formation through statistical evaluation, helping product teams make data-informed choices with confidence.

Fintech sits at the intersection of data and real-world impact, and I'd love to contribute to [Company Name]'s mission by turning raw data into actionable insights. I welcome the opportunity to discuss how my skills align with your team's goals.

Thank you for your time and consideration.

Best regards,
[Your Name]

---

Let me know if you'd like me to tailor it further — for example, by adding a specific company name, adjusting the tone, or exporting it as a document.
results.claude:
Dear Hiring Manager,

I'm excited to apply for the Data Analyst position at your company. With three years of hands-on experience writing SQL to extract, transform, and analyze data, I'm confident I can help your team turn complex datasets into clear, actionable insight — something that feels especially valuable in a fast-moving fintech environment.

In my work, I've built interactive dashboards in Tableau that make key metrics accessible to both technical and non-technical stakeholders, helping teams make faster, better-informed decisions. I also have experience designing and analyzing A/B tests, which has taught me to pair statistical rigor with a focus on the questions that actually move the business forward.

I'd welcome the opportunity to bring this blend of technical skill and product curiosity to your team, and to contribute to data-driven decision-making in a domain where accuracy and trust matter. Thank you for your time and consideration — I'd be glad to discuss how I can help.

Sincerely,
[Your Name]

glm_judge.why: A's prose is smoother and more polished, while both meet intent and are fully accurate.

results.prompt:
Polish this email to a professor asking for a recommendation letter:
hey prof, i took your ML class last sem, got an A. applying to grad school, can you write me a rec letter? thanks
results.agent:
Here's a polished version:

---

**Subject:** Recommendation Letter Request – [Your Name]

Dear Professor [Last Name],

I hope this email finds you well. I was a student in your [Course Name/Number] course last semester and really enjoyed it — it solidified my interest in pursuing machine learning at the graduate level. I earned an A in the course and found your lectures on [mention a specific topic or project, if one stands out] particularly impactful.

I'm now applying to [Master's/PhD] programs in [your field] for Fall [year] admission, and I was hoping you might be willing to write a strong letter of recommendation on my behalf. I believe your perspective on my academic abilities would carry real weight with admissions committees.

I'd be happy to send over my CV, statement of purpose, a list of deadlines, and any other materials that would make the process easier on your end. The first deadline is [date], so I wanted to reach out with plenty of lead time.

Thank you for considering this — and regardless, thank you again for a great semester.

Best,
[Your Full Name]
[Your Student ID, if relevant]

---

Let me know if you'd like to adjust the tone (more formal, more personal), add specific details, or tailor it toward a particular type of program.
results.claude:
Subject: Request for a Letter of Recommendation

Dear Professor [Last Name],

I hope you're doing well. I took your Machine Learning course last semester and earned an A, and I really enjoyed and benefited from the class.

I'm now applying to graduate school, and I was wondering whether you would be willing to write me a letter of recommendation. I'd be happy to share my resume, statement of purpose, a list of programs and deadlines, and any other materials that would be helpful. Of course, I completely understand if you're unable to take this on.

Thank you very much for your time and for a great course.

Best regards,
[Your Full Name]
[Your Program / Student ID, if relevant]

glm_judge.why: A provides a fuller, more polished email with subject line and customization notes, better matching the request.

results.prompt: Explain in 3 sentences why prompt engineering matters less than people think.
results.agent: Prompt engineering is often treated as a dark art, but modern models are increasingly robust to ordinary phrasing and don't require incantations to produce good results. What matters far more is the quality and specificity of the underlying task definition, the context you provide, and the constraints you set — not the exact wording of the prompt. In practice, iteration on the problem itself (clear goals, good examples, relevant information) swamps any marginal gain from clever prompt formatting.
results.claude: Prompt engineering matters less than people think because the biggest gains in output quality come from the underlying model's capability and the quality and relevance of the context you provide, not from clever phrasing or magic words. As models get better at understanding intent, they grow more robust to how a request is worded, so elaborate "tricks" that once helped tend to fade in value over time. What reliably matters is being clear about your actual goal, supplying the right information and constraints, and iterating on the result — which is mostly just good communication rather than a specialized skill.

glm_judge.why: Both answers meet the 3‑sentence request, are clear, factually plausible, and contain no fabricated details.
